example: Skimmed the README. Useful framing, but I'd want a number on time-to-first-insight before I bring it to my team.
Bookmarked. The seed-CSV pattern is the right shape; the demo video is the wrong shape.
I keep seeing "synthetic users" pitched as a discovery tool when it's really a hypothesis-pruning tool. Be honest about that.
Reading the methodology section first. The claim isn't "replace recruited panels" — it's "triage your hypotheses before you spend on a panel." That's the honest pitch.
The schema is the interesting bit. The LLM is the boring bit. If the schema travels between studies, this has legs.
I'd test it on a study where I already have the panel data, then compare. Anything else is vibes.
Schema-first. Determinism on. Reproducible runs. The README says all the right words; need to read the code to believe them.
The eval angle is underplayed in the launch — this is actually a structured eval harness for "will users care?"
Cloned the repo. Will run a mini study tomorrow against a synthetic panel I already have.
This is the BBC-panel pattern formalised. We've done this internally with much worse tooling for a decade.
The brief object is what makes it useful. Predictions tied to a launch slot, with rules — that's how strategy desks actually think.
Sent this to our methods lead. Quietly hopeful.
Synthetic respondents are not respondents. The launch copy slips between simulation and measurement; that's the part to fix.
Show me the validation against a held-out human panel. Until then, this is a hypothesis generator, not research.
Useful to argue against. Will write the long post on Friday.
Public take: thoughtful seed schema. Private take: we're shipping the same primitive; the moat is the prompt + the report.
Their narrator stage is more grown-up than I expected. We need to revisit ours.
Won't engage publicly. Watching the GitHub issues page.
oh this is hot — synthetic audience that shows up in a slide. dropping it in tomorrow's pitch deck.
the BBC panel example is perfect for client decks. literally the pitch.
slack thread to my team: "we have to play with this this week"
Reading the methodology before commenting. The claims about "prediction" need to be unpacked carefully.
There are two distinct objects here: a sampling tool and a generation tool. Conflating them is a category error the field keeps making.
A measured thread is in order. Probably Tuesday after I've read the schema and run the seed.
Loving the Neo4j integration. Inspectable graphs are the difference between a toy and a tool.
The graph_id-per-study pattern is the right design — keeps studies isolated without forking the database.
Will try plugging our salience scorer in. If the runner accepts a custom scorer, this is mine.
Considered. The product is strongest when grounded in a brief — that mirrors how commissioning briefs travel between strategy decks.
Sent the launch link to two old colleagues at JTBC and SBS — curious whether either pilots it.
The narrator section's tone is right for an exec deck. That alone determines whether tools like this get adopted.
